content: otherwise have difficulty making sense of how a dozen different Methuselah feel about and relate to each other - that is, any Storyteller who's not a Methuselah herself! Theme "The truth will out" is the first theme in Lair of the Hidden. No secret lasts forever. The Twelve have hid for more than four can tries, but the players' characters will discover them. This can happen in several different ways. Indeed, one of the Twelve might draw the coterie co the castle as pare of a con to lu ted scheme, thinking that he can control the consequences of this act. More importantly, the coterie may dis cover the ghastly means by which the Twelve guarantee Their secrecy and protection. That revelation can doom the Twelve. The self styled ln con nu have hidden from rh ems el yes as well as the world. They refuse co see how far they have fallen from a noble past. One way or another, the characters force a choice on the Twelve, to confront the horror of their deeds and thepetly spite that divides Them, or to let that spice destroy them. The castle itself is a place of secrets. The Twelve are not what they seem; some of them remain in hiding as long as they can. The Twelve also possess lore unknown to ocher Kindred, from personal memories of Carthage Lo the parables of Saulot. For the greatest secret of all, two entities whose power dwarfs even the nconnu dwell in the castle - and the Twelve know of only one of them. The clash between means and ends pro video the second theme to Lair of the Hidden. The Twelve believe they do good. In fact, they chink they can save the world and stop Gehenna. To pr ore cl themselves, however, they commit cold-blooded murder. The bur den of their sin, as much as their confinement, drives them to the brink of madness. The characters face their own tests of con• science. Hun edo ara holds the promise of great power, from the Twelve and other sources. That power, however, carries a price. As the characters learn the perils and promises of the Twelve, they muse ask themselves how far they will go for the sake of power, know edge ... or, perhaps, mere survival.

Knowing his fate should he be taken captive and unwilling to go down without a fight, Roth riel cured his attention to the unnatural creatures whose talons gripped his flesh. His Eye opened one last time, and he sent his power radiating out from his body to theirs in waves of burning, fiery agony. Screaming in pain and fury, the gargoyles let go their hold on him and then, heedless of the shouts of their master, the enraged beasts literally core him apart. Neither the gargoyles nor the Tremere survived their victory, but there was little satisfaction in their destruction. content: Hun edo ara Ca5Tle:The Grand Tour Guests to the castle may be given a tour of its more public areas by Lady Danika soon after their arrival, if they show any interest. The Tzimisce countess is still very proud of her home; it is her castle, and she has worked hard to keep it running these past few centuries, sometimes under very difficult circumstances. The castle's 50-odd rooms can be divided into three classes: public, where any resident, mortal or undead, may safely go; the various rooms allotted to the mortal servants of the Twelve, which the ln con nu usually enter only to give instructions or co feed; and lase, the private rooms of the ln con nu themselves, which serve as their personal havens and are generally treated by the Twelve as inviolate, except when they are't. F First lm press ions: Arriving At Hun edo ara The main entrance to the castle is via a high bridge that crosses the river far below. Ac the start of the bridge, rh ere's a small, long-neglected shrine. The carved figure of the saint within is cracked and worn by the weather; it's not dear at first exactly which saint it is. Anyone examining the saint more closely, however, will see the saint is wearing armor and has a sword strapped to his side. His hair is long and tied back in an elaborate knot; he has a neatly trimmed beard, aristocratic features and a third eye in the center of his forehead. This is a representation of the Salubri warrior Roth riel, placed here by Danika co honor his memory. irothriel died on the castle's bridge. There is a hidden compartment in the base of the shrine, holding a small brass urn that contains some of the Salubri's ashes. The bridge is easily wide enough For a horse and cart; for pedestrians it's more than adequate. The castle's gate is normally locked with a great iron portcullis. If the guests are expected, however, the portcullis will be raised, and torches lit within, welcoming them inside. There's a dank, unpleasant odor as the characters pass through the gatehouse, though: the fa in reek of omen hi no dead, of mildew and rot. The floor of the gatehouse is littered with debris; rank straw, dead leaves., small branches and an occasional fragment of bone. Off to one side, Uwiu Trio: Wet~ 1H[ Was 35

content: enough to endure repeated feedings, medieval live ing conditions, illness and the annual lottery, though some have managed co last much longer. The her van cs' hierarchy has as much to do with seniority as much as an individual's specific duties, so those who do manage co survive can expect the dubious reward of rising in it, perhaps even earning more pres ti pious responsibilities than when they first started. The highest-status servants arc those who personally serve one of the Twelve. Their word is law to their follows, and their requests treated as if one of the masters themselves were asking it. They sleep not in the common dormitories, but in more private quarters with their respective master, or ne oft he rooms on the third floor. They move about the castle free! y by day or by night, and do not seem to fear even the monstrous Sc lav. They also leave the castle on errands for their masters, sometimes even going as far as Deva or even Bucharest ... a freedom few of the common servants enjoy. On the rare occasions that one of the personal servants is selected in the lottery, or dies due to some ocher reason, the servants indulge in considerable speculation over - and even competition for - her position. The personal servants know their lot is no better, and in some ways worse. 17,air lives hang on their master's whim and their ability to satisfy them that is all chat fills their minds most of the time. They have no more privacy than their fellows if they share their master's rooms, only a cot and small chest In an out of che way corner. They feed on their master's blood and are often addicted co the Kiss, which serves only to underscore their desire to please. They are often privy to some of their mas tears' secrets, or overhear conversations, arguments and bitter insults traded among the Twelve, which can put their lives in danger should one of the ln con nu decide they know too much. Not all the Twelve have much patience for less than perfect service, so their servants must learn quickly and well what is expected of them. The castle's seneschal, loan Srurza, reports di erectly to Danika and as such holds rank equal with the servants of other ln con nu. His responsibilities, however, are focused less on her personally, and more toward the castle as a whole. loan oversees all the other servants, assigns duties, checks work equal it, settles disputes, and makes sure everything is running smoothly so his mistress is not troubled by domestic problems. He also handles the acquisition of necessary provisions, everything from foodstuffs for the servants' kitchen co silk embroidery floss for Law or 11l Hioool 54

content: Se cite prefers co drop hints through questions of his own. For instance, Pentweret would not say, "Only Cree and Hill could make the sort of talisman you describe." Ln stead he would S/my, "That talisman in't like my Egyptian magic. I don't put parchment in lockets, I engrave gems. I wonder what tradition docs make talismans like that?" And then he would refer the characters to the castle's library. If the characters grow impatient with Pentweret's oblique hints, the Setite suggests that the characters can only crust the answers they find for themselves. Anyone who offers them a complete answer and accusation might be trying to frame someone else, no? By helping the characters, Pentwerct shows off his own moral super• ority - and keeps the pack from accusing him. Before too long, The Twelve move from slander to bribery. Some of them - as many as the Storyteller thinks appropriate-offer favors if the pack will finger an enemy as their attacker. Once the characters realize that either Cree or Hill attacked them and the bishop, the two Ventrue offer the greatest bribes of all. These two master th au mat urges can give the characters ex traordinary magical treasures and services, iron I they will blame someone else. W t'Re'Glad The rest of the Twelve gladly support whatever accusation the pack makes in the end. Cree or Hill plead for mercy: Hill on the grounds that the slain bishop served the Antediluvians, while Cree claims first that he me rely sought to protect his childe, and that he actually wanted to draw the characters in co punish his childe. Anyone else accused me rely insists chat the Sabbat have the wrong Caintte, and that Creator Hill is co blame. If the characters accuse Danika herself,she takes chis as an insult to her hospitality and orders the destruction of the entire pack. Some of the Twelve needle her for the breach ofrrust, but the Sc lav obeys her. Both Cree and Hill join in carrying out her sentence. For anyone else, though, the other ln con nu restrain che accused elder so che pack can impose whatever punishment they choose - so,t of Final Death or torpor. Pr !Cl; Of t'Age'Once' 11,e characters may reject Danika's offer of a were gild and try to punish Crew, Hill or whoever else they think attacked them and the bishop. They can even recruit others among the Twelve to help them if they conceal their actual intent to kill their target. Methuselah become easier to kill when you have the help of other Methuselah.

If the character follows the spectral maiden, she walks to a spot on the castle drawbridge, falls co her knees and silently sobs For a mom cm, then vanishes. content: The demon also possesses the crucial power to defend the Twelve from each ocher. Any accack by one pact er upon anorheraffecrs the attacker as well as the victim. lfoneo( the Twelve is de sc roy ed, his killer also meets Final Death. None of the Twelve may leave the castle, on pain o( breaking the Pace. The demon insisted on this condition to keep the Twelve under its mecca phorical thumb. The original Twelve agreed as a necessary hedge against betrayal and accidents. If some freakish ci rcumscance cook a packer out of the castle grounds against his will, che Pack grants him Io minutes to return before che demon imposes the sanction of Final Death. This incidentally breaks the Pace. Servants suffer a weaker version of this ban: They may leave the castle on errands, but the demon can destroy them if they cry to leave for good. Guests may leave any me they want. .. aside from the Sc lav sleeping in the doorway, blood bonds of loyalty and any curses imposed by the Twelve. Once per year, on the anniversary of the Pace, the Twelve must renew the contract by offering the demon a human san if ice. The sacrifice muse take place between dusk and midnight. All 12 packers must accent. The officiating vampire curs the victim's throat and pours the victim's blood into a fire. Each packer also sheds a drop of his own vice. If the sacrifice does not race place in the allotted time, or all 12 do nor attend and affirm their desire co continue the Pace, the demon can claim their souls. The Pact does not cake account of s~dial circumstances such as a pact er completely claimed by the Beast and no longer able to speak. On the other hand, the Pact includes some ambiguous phrases that enabled the Twelve to replace a member who lost her human intellect. The demon demands that each of the last year's packers appear, blood and soul, co renew the con tract. When the packer Van th lost her mind and d1e capacity of pee ch, the remaining packers per shaded their accidental guest Buff to diablerie the mad Med1Uselah. Thus, Van th's blood and soul attended the ceremony although Van ch herself was dust. Some of the Twelve believe the terms of the Pact permit less drastic means of replacement as well. 146
